result:
Here is what comes to me.

We are living in the age of the parenthesis. Everything—our attention, our politics, our sense of time, even our empathy—feels suspended between an opening clause we can barely remember and a closing period we cannot yet see. The great before, and the unknown after. We are in the long, uncomfortable, stuttering middle of something, and the grammar of our lives has gone wobbly.

I look out my window. The tree there is neither fully in winter’s sleep nor in spring’s riot. It is in the *becoming*. Buds are hard, tight-fisted promises against a grey sky. That’s us. All potential, coiled with anxiety, waiting for the signal to unfurl into a shape we don’t yet know. We scroll through feeds that are digital campfires, telling each other stories of impending doom or miraculous utopias, both equally fantastical. We are hungry for a narrative with a clear arc, but the plot has thickened into sludge.

I think about a conversation overheard in a café, two friends leaning in. “I’m just… tired,” one said, not of work, but of the *atmosphere*. The psychic weather. It’s a low-pressure system stretching across the globe, a feeling of charged uncertainty. Will it storm? Will it dissipate? No one knows. So we carry our internal barometers everywhere, watching them flicker.

This is the time of hybrids. We are digital creatures in animal bodies, craving touch while crafting perfect online avatars. We work from bedrooms that are also offices, on sofas that are also conference rooms. The boundaries have melted, and while that can mean freedom, it also means nothing ever really ends. The work day, the news cycle, the anxiety—it all just bleeds, a watercolor left in the rain.

And yet. And yet, in this parenthesis, I notice small, defiant acts of meaning-making. A neighbor plants bulbs in the autumn, a pure act of faith in a future self she will be, in a spring she will see. A friend learns to bake sourdough, not because it’s trendy, but because the slow, tactile process of fermentation—of waiting and tending—feels like a truth our fast world has forgotten. The dough rises in its own time. It cannot be hurried by a notification.

There is a strange tenderness that blooms in parentheses. When you know you’re in a transitional space, you pay closer attention. You notice the quality of the light at 4 p.m., the way it gilds the dust motes. You savor the mundane chat with the barista because it is an anchor in the day’s drift. You reread a favorite book, not for plot, but for the comfort of known cadences, the familiar voice in your head. In the absence of a grand narrative, we assemble mosaics of minute beauties and kindnesses. They are our rebellion against the void.

I think of the medieval monks who, in times of plague and upheaval, painstakingly illuminated manuscripts. While the world outside convulsed, they focused on the curve of a single letter, filling it with lapis blue and gold leaf. They were preserving not just text, but care, attention, a testament that beauty was worth making even (especially) when the horizon was dark. Our modern illuminations might be a perfectly crafted playlist for a friend, a well-tended community garden in a vacant lot, a patient explanation to a child of why the sky is blue.

Perhaps the challenge of our parenthesis is to stop waiting for the main sentence to resume. This *is* the sentence. The fragmented, run-on, comma-spliced, beautiful mess of it. This is where we live. In the waiting room, we can either count the faded magazines, or we can start talking to the others waiting with us. We can build a temporary community of the displaced, sharing stories and snacks, making the liminal space feel, for a moment, like a destination in itself.

We are forgetting how to be bored. To stare out a train window without a screen, to let the mind wander without a goal. Boredom is the blank page before the poem, the silence before the music. In our parenthesis, we fill every second with input, terrified of the quiet that might whisper a question we don’t want to answer: *What do you truly want? What are you building, here in the in-between?*

So here is a proposal, for myself and for anyone resonating with this feeling. What if we treated the parenthesis not as a cage, but as a cocoon? A necessary, transformative confinement. The caterpillar doesn’t know it will be a butterfly; it just follows an instinct to dissolve into goo, trusting a deep, cellular blueprint. Our blueprint is human. It’s for connection, creation, and care.

Let’s be gentle with our gooey, mid-metamorphosis selves. Let’s build sanctuaries of slowness. Let’s have conversations that don’t conclude, but meander. Let’s plant things we might not be around to see flower. Let’s write letters on paper, tangible artifacts of this time, to be found in a drawer by someone we will never meet.

The parenthesis will close. History’s punctuation is inevitable. But when it does, what will we have been, in here? Will we have been merely anxious passengers, white-knuckled until the ride resumed? Or will we have been quiet gardeners of our tiny, shared plot, tending to what we could, making meaning from the mulch and the sunlight we were given?

The tree outside my window doesn’t fret about the timeline of its budding. It gathers the weak winter light, draws water from the cold earth, and prepares. It is doing its tree-work, in tree-time. Our human-work, in this human-time, might be simpler than we think. To look each other in the eye. To listen past the first sentence. To create one small, undeniable thing of beauty. To be kind, especially when it feels naïve. To hold the line for truth. To wait, not passively, but like a plant waits: actively, organically, rooted in the real.

The light is shifting now. The grey sky is softening to a pearlescent pink at the edges. The parenthesis is still open. But inside it, the world is humming, waiting, alive. And so are we. Let’s make of this awkward, precious, suspended moment, a home.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
